_comment: Clinical decision pathway for Acute Otitis Media (ear infection). Based on NICE CG60.
label: Acute Otitis Media (Ear Infection)

questions.text: How long have you had ear pain?
Do you have discharge coming from your ear?
Do you have a fever (temperature above 38°C)?
Do you have dizziness or problems with your balance?
Do you have any facial weakness or drooping?
Do you have swelling or redness behind the ear?
Have you had any recent hearing loss in the affected ear?
Do you have a perforated eardrum (diagnosed previously)?

redFlags.description: Facial nerve weakness — possible facial nerve palsy from inner ear infection
redFlags.message: Facial weakness is an emergency. Call 999 immediately.
Swelling behind the ear may indicate mastoiditis. Go to A&E immediately.

redFlags.description: Dizziness with fever — possible inner ear infection or labyrinthitis
redFlags.message: Dizziness and fever together need urgent medical assessment today.

eligibilityRules.reason: Ear discharge with known perforated eardrum — requires GP assessment before treatment
Mild symptoms under 3 days without fever — self-care first approach recommended

outcomeRules.reason: Mild early-stage ear infection — watchful waiting with self-care
Acute otitis media eligible for pharmacy-led treatment

selfCareAdvice: Paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain relief. Warm compress against the ear. Most ear infections resolve within 3 days without antibiotics.
pharmacyTreatmentOptions: Amoxicillin 500mg three times daily for 5 days
Clarithromycin 500mg twice daily for 5 days (penicillin allergy)
safetyNetAdvice: Return urgently if: swelling behind the ear, facial weakness, dizziness, or if a child becomes very unwell.
